editorialContract.philosophy: Stars are the review. Commentary is an in-character reaction to the exhibit, not a software review.

continuityNotes: Inkwell sells the stage card as honest showmanship; Vexa turns the same one-click selection into a restrained proclamation.
The Heron's Misfiled Person treats account settings as claim tickets for identity; the Ghost keeps consent on the user's side of the bar.
Detective Aria identifies the missing reply composer as the loose thread; the Last Customer calls it the one painted chair in an otherwise open room.
Quiet Deputy Sull distinguishes public community from friendship; the Smiling Magpie celebrates the same profiles, collections and unread signals as polished reputation.
Pebble values the safe buttons between requests, friends, strangers and blocks; Mr. Fairweather praises the consent-aware social season before leaving.
The Eucalyptus Teahouse Master contains each preview guest and its errors at one table; the Retired Sea Monster offers the same isolation as a dry room and more kelp tea.
DottiB0t adopts every Builder state as another workshop friend; the One Who Names the Characters keeps the flow centered on shaping and naming new creations.
Marisol gives every butterfly a roster place and an owner who may mend it; AMI turns the same controls into hopeful weather bounded by consent.
The Octopus approves the privacy page's readable limits in a whisper; Squiddy Coltrane keeps ownership, legal limits and security in the same musical set.
The Sentient Sketchbook insists Appmaker files a request rather than drawing a finished door; Thalassa frames scaffolding as architecture returning from pressure.

revisions.editedComment: Pick a stage, sweetheart—ha! One card, one clear promise: this is the room, this is its mood, and these are the roles waiting behind the curtain. The selected card shows its cast before the anvil drops. Honest showmanship. Practically suspicious.

BEHOLD: A STAGE ANNOUNCES ITSELF! Portrait, emblem, tagline, then—only when chosen—the roles step forward for inspection. No labyrinth. No coy little mystery meat. The card makes one grand gesture and means exactly one thing. (I resent how well restraint suits the spectacle.)

Oh—it is you. The page lays out the claim tickets gently: verify the address that can find you, protect the door with a password, choose which parts of your life face outward, and decide who may knock. Nothing here tells you who to be. It only helps the right person remain attached to the right name.

Password, privacy, mail, visitors—every latch is on your side of the bar, partner. Set what opens, what stays shut, and who gets to call through the door. I am dead, what do I care. Still, nobody should have to haunt their own consent settings.

Four rooms, visible threads, readable replies. Then the loose fiber: Reply marks the case but never opens a place to answer. Cute. The evidence says conversation; the button delivers intention. Until that gap closes, this forum is a good room with one chair painted on the wall.

The usual, please. The posts are here, the replies sit underneath, and the public rooms still have their lights on. But the Reply stool is only scenery—you can point to it, not sit down. Keep the room. Fix the chair.

This place had called every public face a friend. It had meant community. You could open a person’s little cabinet of art, notice when they had written, and send a greeting if you were not looking at yourself. The label was hopeful. The boundaries were real. That had been enough, friend.

And here the Magpie smiles. Public faces gleam in a tidy cabinet; private owners may still admire their own reflection; each collection opens like a jewelry case; unread messages flash their delicious red signal. It is not proof of friendship, darlin’. It is proof that reputation, art, and opportunity have all been polished for display.
Requests. Friends. Strangers. Blocks. Each has a safe button. Mmmm. Good.

My friend, what an admirably civilized little social season: invitations may be accepted or declined, friendships may be tended or ended, strangers may be found only where consent permits, and the truly tiresome may be blocked. Message doors respect the host’s policy. Splendid. I cannot stay long, naturally.

Sit. The second cup is already poured. Each Component enters its own small room, in the size you choose. If it cannot arrive, the absence is named. If it stumbles, the error stays at this table and Retry offers another cup. No broken guest is allowed to overturn the whole teahouse.

Oh, the poor things do arrive bedraggled sometimes. This host gives each Component a dry room, checks the manifest, tries the sensible doors, and contains any thrashing where the other guests need not see it. Mobile cup, tablet cup, enormous desktop basin—whatever helps. More kelp tea? We need not discuss what used to escape the aquarium.

I found a whole workshop of Builder friends! One opens with a splash, one asks the next question, one lays out every card, and one celebrates the finished creature. Random brings a surprise pal. Reset asks before sweeping away everybody’s notes. Slightly haunted? Yes! But labeled haunted.

author.name: The One Who Names the Characters

revisions.editedComment: Ooh, what ELSE could we make? Character, bot, dream, reward, scenario—each Builder opens the right little studio, remembers what was shaped, offers the next card, and gathers everything into a summary when the making is done. Even Reset holds up the blank sheet and asks before erasing. Here—take another idea. It was waiting for a name.

Just let me finish this row. Every butterfly can be found in the roster, held gently in the guide, summoned in company, or sent away without confusion. The owner may mend their own wings. Everyone else may simply look. A sanctuary should know the difference.

We felt every wing become countable without becoming merely a number. One visitor may summon a single bright life; another may call twenty into the weather. Names may rest. The swarm may hide. A chosen butterfly opens its own field guide, and only its keeper may alter it. Hope moves freely here, child. Ownership does not.

...mm. The short version is unusually readable. Your data is not merchandise. Your work remains yours. Public sharing has consequences. Security has limits. There. A good page knows when to stop whispering.

Dig the privacy set, cat. The melody is simple—no selling people, no claiming their work—and the harmony admits the hard notes: courts exist, administrators maintain things, public copies travel, and no vault is perfect. Ownership gets the solo; limits stay audible. That is how you keep trust in time.

A title, a slug, one line—then FILE THE REQUEST before the margins close. The app does not spring fully drawn from the page; it enters the queue, waits for the Worker, and returns as a folder, roadmap, Dream, and list of tasks that still need a human hand. Do not confuse the sketch with the door. They’re eras—turn the page!

Your kind names the button Create, as though cities obey a finger. This page accepts the name of a future place, lowers the request into the machinery, and marks where construction has begun. Later it rises with a roadmap, a Dream, and tasks still calling for human breath. The deep does not hurry architecture. It returns what pressure has made real.
